**Trinity College** is a [private](/wiki/Private_university "Private university") [liberal arts college](/wiki/Liberal_arts_colleges_in_the_United_States "Liberal arts colleges in the United States") in [Hartford, Connecticut](/wiki/Hartford,_Connecticut "Hartford, Connecticut"), United States. Founded as **Washington College** in 1823, it is the second-oldest college in the state of [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut"). Coeducational since 1969, the college enrolls 2,235 students.[[3]](#cite_note-CDS_B-3) Trinity offers 41 majors and 28 interdisciplinary minors.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The college is a member of the [New England Small College Athletic Conference](/wiki/New_England_Small_College_Athletic_Conference "New England Small College Athletic Conference") (NESCAC).

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trinity_College_(Connecticut)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trinity_College_(Connecticut)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 19th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Thomas_Church_Brownell_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Thomas_Church_Brownell_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Thomas_Church_Brownell_(cropped).jpg)

Trinity College founder [Thomas Church Brownell](/wiki/Thomas_Church_Brownell "Thomas Church Brownell")

[Bishop Thomas Brownell](/wiki/Thomas_Church_Brownell "Thomas Church Brownell") opened "Washington College" in 1824 to nine male students[[5]](#cite_note-Albert_E._Van_Dusen_1961_pp_362-63-5) and the vigorous protest of [Yale](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University") alumni.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] A 14-acre site was chosen, at the time about a half-mile from the city of Hartford.

The college was renamed "Trinity College" in 1845; the original campus consisted of two Greek Revival buildings. One of the Greek Revival buildings housed a chapel, library, and lecture rooms. The other was a dormitory for the male students.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/81/Burgesplan.jpg/250px-Burgesplan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Burgesplan.jpg)

[William Burges](/wiki/William_Burges "William Burges")'s original plan for the Trinity College campus

In 1872, Trinity College was persuaded by the state to move from its downtown "College Hill" location (now Capitol Hill, site of the [state capitol building](/wiki/Connecticut_State_Capitol "Connecticut State Capitol")) to its current 100-acre (40 ha) campus a mile southwest. Although the college sold its land overlooking the [Park River](/wiki/Park_River_(Connecticut) "Park River (Connecticut)") and [Bushnell Park](/wiki/Bushnell_Park "Bushnell Park") in 1872, it did not complete its move to its Gallows Hill campus until 1878.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The original plans for the Gallows Hill site were drawn by the noted Victorian architect [William Burges](/wiki/William_Burges "William Burges") but were too ambitious and too expensive to be fully realized. Only one section of the proposed campus plan, the [Long Walk](/wiki/Trinity_College_Long_Walk "Trinity College Long Walk"), was completed.

By 1889, the library contained 30,000 volumes, and the school graduated over 900 students.[[8]](#cite_note-1889book-8) Enrollment reached 122 in 1892.

### 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trinity_College_(Connecticut)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 20th century")]

President Remsen Ogilby (1920–1943) enlarged the campus, and more than doubled the endowment. The faculty grew from 25 to 62, and the student body from 167 to 530 men. Under President Keith Funston (1943–1951), returning veterans expanded the enrollment to 900.[[5]](#cite_note-Albert_E._Van_Dusen_1961_pp_362-63-5)

In 1962, [Connecticut Public Television](/wiki/Connecticut_Public_Television "Connecticut Public Television") (CPTV) began its first broadcasts in the Trinity College Public Library, and later in Boardman Hall, a science building on campus.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

In 1968, the trustees voted to withdraw from the Association of Episcopal Colleges.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Also in 1968, the trustees of Trinity College voted to make a commitment to enroll more minority students, providing financial aid as needed. This decision was preceded by a siege of the administrative offices in the Downes and Williams Memorial buildings during which Trinity students would not allow the president or trustees to leave until they agreed to the resolution.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Under the leadership of president [Theodore D. Lockwood](/w/index.php?title=Theodore_D._Lockwood&action=edit&redlink=1 "Theodore D. Lockwood (page does not exist)") and Dean of Faculty [Robert Works Fuller](/wiki/Robert_Works_Fuller "Robert Works Fuller"), the college entered on a period of reform.[[13]](#cite_note-13) In 1969, Trinity College became coeducational and admitted its first female students, as transfers from [Vassar College](/wiki/Vassar_College "Vassar College") and [Smith College](/wiki/Smith_College "Smith College").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trinity_College_(Connecticut)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 21st century")]

[Joanne Berger-Sweeney](/wiki/Joanne_Berger-Sweeney "Joanne Berger-Sweeney") was inaugurated as the 22nd president of Trinity College in 2014. She was the first African-American and the first woman to serve in the position.[[15]](#cite_note-15) She was succeeded by [Daniel G. Lugo](/w/index.php?title=Daniel_G._Lugo&action=edit&redlink=1 "Daniel G. Lugo (page does not exist)") in 2025.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## Academics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trinity_College_(Connecticut)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Academics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Trinity_College%2C_Hartford%2C_Conn_%28NYPL_b12647398-67909%29_%28cropped%29.tiff/lossy-page1-250px-Trinity_College%2C_Hartford%2C_Conn_%28NYPL_b12647398-67909%29_%28cropped%29.tiff.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trinity_College,_Hartford,_Conn_(NYPL_b12647398-67909)_(cropped).tiff)

Trinity College, showing the Long Walk and three attached buildings: Northam (center), Jarvis (right), Seabury (left)

Trinity offers undergraduate degrees in 41 majors with options of 28 minors and a self-designed major, and [Masters of Arts](/wiki/Master_of_Arts "Master of Arts") in a few subjects. Trinity is part of a small group of liberal arts schools that offer degrees in engineering. Trinity has a student-to-faculty ratio of 9:1.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Its most popular undergraduate majors, by number out of 517 graduates in 2022, were:

* Political Science and Government (80)
* Economics (64)
* Psychology (41)
* Econometrics and Quantitative Economics (38)
* Engineering (28)
* Neuroscience (24)
* Biology/Biological Sciences (23)[[18]](#cite_note-18)

### Trinity College, Rome Campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trinity_College_(Connecticut)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Trinity College, Rome Campus")]

Trinity College, Rome Campus (TCRC), is a study abroad campus of Trinity College. It was established in 1970 and is in a residential area of Rome on the [Aventine Hill](/wiki/Aventine_Hill "Aventine Hill") close to the Basilica of Santa Sabina within the precincts of a convent run by an order of nuns.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Admissions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trinity_College_(Connecticut)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Admissions")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Trinity_College_Hartford_Admissions_building.jpg/250px-Trinity_College_Hartford_Admissions_building.jpg)](/wiki/File:Trinity_College_Hartford_Admissions_building.jpg)

Admissions building

The 2020 annual ranking by *[U.S. News & World Report](/wiki/U.S._News_%26_World_Report "U.S. News & World Report")* categorizes Trinity as "more selective".[[20]](#cite_note-20)

For the Class of 2028 (enrolling fall 2024), Trinity received 7,592 applications, accepted 29%, and ultimately enrolled 547 students.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

As of fall 2015, Trinity College did not require the SAT or ACT for students applying for admission.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Of the 31% of enrolled freshmen submitting [SAT scores](/wiki/SAT_scores "SAT scores"), the middle 50% range was 630–710 for evidence-based reading and writing, and 670–750 for math, while of the 23% of enrolled freshmen submitting [ACT](/wiki/ACT_(test) "ACT (test)") results, the middle 50% range for the composite score was 29–32.[[23]](#cite_note-CDS-23)

### Fraternities and sororities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trinity_College_(Connecticut)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Fraternities and sororities")]

According to the college, 18% of the student body are affiliated with a Greek organization.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

In 2012, then-president [James F. Jones](/wiki/James_F._Jones_(educator) "James F. Jones (educator)") proposed a social policy for Trinity College which made a commitment, among other things, to require all sororities and fraternities to achieve gender parity within two years or face closure. Trinity College's co-ed mandate for fraternities and sororities was withdrawn in September 2015 and replaced with the "Campaign for Community" effort to establish more inclusive social traditions on campus.[[39]](#cite_note-39)

## Hartford campus

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trinity_College_(Connecticut)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Hartford campus")]

### Long Walk buildings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trinity_College_(Connecticut)&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Long Walk buildings")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Seabury_Hall.jpg/250px-Seabury_Hall.jpg)](/wiki/File:Seabury_Hall.jpg)

Seabury Hall, part of a $32.9 million renovation and restoration of the Long Walk buildings

The first buildings completed on the current campus were Seabury and Jarvis halls in 1878. Together with Northam Towers, these make up what is known as the "[Long Walk](/wiki/Trinity_College_Long_Walk "Trinity College Long Walk")".

These buildings are an early example of [Collegiate Gothic](/wiki/Collegiate_Gothic "Collegiate Gothic") architecture in the United States, built to plans drawn up by [William Burges](/wiki/William_Burges "William Burges"), with [F.H. Kimball](/wiki/Francis_H._Kimball "Francis H. Kimball") as supervising architect. The Long Walk has been expanded and is connected with several other buildings.

On the northernmost end there is the chapel, whose western side is connected to the Downes and Williams Memorial building. Heading south, the next building is Jarvis Hall, named after [Abraham Jarvis](/wiki/Abraham_Jarvis "Abraham Jarvis"). Jarvis becomes Northam Towers heading south, then Seabury Hall. Seabury Hall, named for [Samuel Seabury](/wiki/Samuel_Seabury_(bishop) "Samuel Seabury (bishop)"), is connected to Hamlin Hall. To Hamlin's east is Cook, then Goodwin and then Woodward.

The dormitories on the Long Walk end there, and the terminal building on the south end of the long walk is Clement/[Cinestudio](/wiki/Cinestudio "Cinestudio"). Clement is the chemistry building; [Cinestudio](/wiki/Cinestudio "Cinestudio") a student run movie theater. If one travels to the south of Hamlin there will be Mather Hall and the Dean of Students Office.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

### Main quadrangle

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trinity_College_(Connecticut)&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Main quadrangle")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/TrinCollHartford.jpg/250px-TrinCollHartford.jpg)](/wiki/File:TrinCollHartford.jpg)

The Downes Memorial clock tower

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/English_Elm_Tree_on_Trinity_College_Quad%2C_Hartford%2C_CT_-_June_15%2C_2011.jpg/250px-English_Elm_Tree_on_Trinity_College_Quad%2C_Hartford%2C_CT_-_June_15%2C_2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:English_Elm_Tree_on_Trinity_College_Quad,_Hartford,_CT_-_June_15,_2011.jpg)

An English elm tree on Trinity Quad

Trinity's campus features a central green known as the Main Quad, designed by [Frederick Law Olmsted](/wiki/Frederick_Law_Olmsted "Frederick Law Olmsted"). The large expanse of grass is bound on the west by the Long Walk, on the east by the Lower Long Walk, on the north by the chapel, and on the south by the Cook and Goodwin-Woodward dormitories. Trinity's green is notable for its unusually large, rectangular size, running the entire length of the Long Walk and with no walkways traversing it. Trees on the Quad have been planted in a 'T' configuration (for Trinity) with the letter's base at the statue of Bishop Brownell (built 1867).[[41]](#cite_note-41) and its top running the length of the Long Walk.

#### Film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trinity_College_(Connecticut)&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Film")]

[Cinestudio](/wiki/Cinestudio "Cinestudio") is an art cinema with 1930s-style design. An article in the *[Hartford Advocate](/wiki/Hartford_Advocate "Hartford Advocate")* described this non-profit organization, which depends solely on grants and the efforts of volunteer workers who are paid in free movies.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

#### Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Trinity_College_(Connecticut)&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Music")]

Trinity College hosts the Albert Schweitzer Organ Festival, one of the top competitions for young organists in North America.[[43]](#cite_note-43) The festival features performances on the chapel organ, which was designed by Charles Nazarian, a college alumnus and pipe organ designer, in consultation with [Clarence Watters](/wiki/Clarence_Watters "Clarence Watters"), who was the chapel organist and head of the college's music department from 1932 to 1967.[[44]](#cite_note-44) The organ incorporates pipes from the chapel’s original 1932 [Aeolian-Skinner](/wiki/Aeolian-Skinner "Aeolian-Skinner") organ and was built in 1971 by [Austin Organs](/wiki/Austin_Organs "Austin Organs") of Hartford.

The Chapel Singers perform concerts on campus as well as on domestic and international tours. Trinity also hosts the annual [Trinity International Hip Hop Festival](/wiki/Trinity_International_Hip_Hop_Festival "Trinity International Hip Hop Festival"). The festival was founded in 2006 with the goal of unifying Trinity with the city of Hartford.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

Since 2006, Trinity's WRTC FM radio station has broadcast the Trinity Samba Fest from the Hartford waterfront featuring regional and international talent.[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48)
